title: The nature of product
description: A very interesting interview to Marty Cagan. Very good insights about empowered teams, product management, and team organization.
notes:
Here are the most important points from Marty Cagan's presentation:
- **The fundamental difference between feature teams and real product teams:** Feature teams are handed a roadmap of prioritized features (solutions) to build, primarily serving the business. In contrast, real product teams are given problems to solve (customer or company problems) and are empowered to come up with the best solutions. Real product teams focus on outcomes (solving problems) rather than output (releasing features).
- **The drastically different role of the product manager:** In feature teams, the product manager often acts as a project manager or "cat herder," gathering requirements and ensuring delivery. In empowered product teams, the product manager is responsible for discovering solutions that are valuable and viable, working as a peer with designers (focused on usability) and engineers (focused on feasibility). The job is so different that Cagan suggests they shouldn't even share the same title.
- **Misconceptions about product management:** Many people mistakenly believe that product managers primarily write requirements (PRDs, user stories) and hand them off to designers and engineers for implementation. They also often think the job is just about cranking out features based on a roadmap. Cagan emphasizes that product management in successful companies involves deep collaboration and bringing unique skills to the team beyond just defining what to build.
- **Don't overinvest in problem validation:** While understanding the problem is important, **people buy solutions, not the problem**. Product teams should be careful not to spend excessive time validating a problem, especially if it's already well-understood, and instead focus on discovering a winning solution.
- **The true purpose of user research:** User research is not just about confirming that users like a prototype. The primary goal is to find all the reasons why users _won't_ use the product. It's evaluative, helping to identify flaws and areas for improvement. Furthermore, **product managers and designers must actively participate in user research sessions**; reports alone are often ignored.
- **Transitioning from a feature team to a product team is possible:** Individuals in feature teams can try a small experiment, proposing to their manager that their team operate as a product team for a quarter or two, focusing on solving problems and measuring success by outcomes.
- **Key skills for a product manager in an empowered team:** A product manager needs to be an expert in four areas: understanding users and customers, being an expert in the data (product usage, analytics), understanding all parts of the business (marketing, sales, monetization, compliance), and knowing the competitive landscape and industry trends. They also need to ensure the team has strategic context (product vision and strategy) and learn product discovery skills and techniques.
- **Concerns about the evolution of product management:** Cagan is worried about the proliferation of unqualified "product people," particularly in Europe with the misapplication of the "product owner" role by agile coaches lacking product experience. He also sees issues with poorly implemented product ops that might interfere with the core responsibilities of a product manager.
- **The three sacred responsibilities of a product manager:** These must never be delegated: **unencumbered access to users and customers, unencumbered access to engineers, and unencumbered access to stakeholders**. Companies that try to split the product manager role by limiting these accesses create significant damage.
- **The danger of scaling with process over leaders:** While scaling is challenging, relying too heavily on processes (like SAFe) instead of developing strong leaders is a significant risk and can lead to "repackaged waterfall" that is the antithesis of agile principles. Cagan echoes Steve Jobs' warning about the "disease of process people".
- **Marty Cagan welcomes hard questions:** He finds them inspiring for his writing and encourages people to email him with challenging product management questions they can't find good answers to. His resources can be found at svpg.com.

title: The Marty Cagan special - ProductTank #27 Singapore
description: An open discussion on Modern Product Management.  The talk contains a lot of interesting discussions during the Q&A. I think it covers all the underpinnings of modern product management.
notes:
True product management requires moving beyond administrative "product owner" tasks to become an empowered leader who solves business and customer problems through deep knowledge and rapid discovery. Success is measured by achieving meaningful outcomes and creating value, rather than simply delivering a roadmap of features.

*   **Product Manager vs. Product Owner:** The Product Owner role is primarily administrative and represents only about 10% of a Product Manager's job. A true Product Manager requires deep skill-building—often taking one to two years—ideally through coaching by experienced mentors.
*   **Core Competencies:** A successful Product Manager must possess deep knowledge in four specific areas: users/customers, data analytics, the business (including finance, legal, and sales constraints), and the industry (competitors and technology trends).
*   **The Four Risks:** Modern product management focuses on tackling four key risks upfront: Value (will customers buy or use it?), Usability (can they figure out how to use it?), Feasibility (can we build it with our current technology and time?), and Viability (does the solution work for our business?).
*   **Discovery vs. Delivery:** Discovery is the process of finding a solution that is valuable, usable, feasible, and viable using prototypes. Delivery is the separate effort of building a production-quality, scalable, and reliable product.
*   **Outcome over Output:** Success is measured by solving business problems (outcomes) rather than simply shipping features (output). OKRs should focus on business results, while traditional roadmaps of activities often fail to solve the intended problems.
*   **Collaborative Innovation:** True innovation rarely comes from customers or requirements documents; it typically comes from engineers who understand what is "just now possible" with technology. PMs, designers, and engineers must collaborate side-by-side rather than through a hand-off of requirements.
*   **Value Creation vs. Value Capture:** Product teams should focus the majority of their efforts on creating new value (discovery) rather than just capturing existing value through low-risk optimizations and minor tweaks.
*   **High-Frequency Iteration:** Effective discovery involves rapid experimentation. Great teams may test 50 to 200 ideas a month, often performing 15 to 30 iterations per week using prototypes to find a winning solution.
*   **Deep Work Requirement:** To perform the actual Product Manager role, an individual needs at least four hours of "deep work" per day, free from meetings and administrative project management tasks.
*   **Continuous Customer Research:** Teams should conduct at least three one-hour sessions with users every week to identify pain points and understand why they might *not* use a product.
*   **Role of Leadership:** The most important responsibility of product leaders (Managers, Directors, VPs) is coaching their Product Managers to become skilled. They are also responsible for setting the vision, strategy, and objectives that align multiple teams.
*   **Ethical Responsibility:** Beyond technical and business risks, product people must evaluate ethical risk—asking "should we build it?"—to consider the potential negative side effects on society and customers.

title: GeePaw Hill on Incremental Software Delivery
description: Pure wisdom on why working in small, safe steps is the most efficient way to work in software product development when we have environments of high uncertainty (which is almost always).
notes:
Drawing on the information in the YouTube video transcript, here are the most important points of GeePaw Hill's presentation on Incremental Software Delivery:

\*  \*\*The core argument of the presentation is that you should probably take many more much smaller steps when making changes, particularly when your app is already shipping.\*\* This is presented as a default and powerful piece of advice.

\*  Hill defines a \*\*"step" as a gap in time between two points of "readiness".\*\* At the "before" point, you are ready; during the step, you are unready; and at the "after" point, you are ready again.

\*  \*\*"Ready" means that at any given point, you have source code from which you can regenerate your running app without concerns.\*\* This definition explicitly excludes the requirement that the new program be better than the previous one and also ignores the cost of deployment. The focus is solely on whether deployment would cause a problem.

\*  The traditional approach often involves \*\*very large steps\*\*, potentially lasting weeks or even a month (e.g., 30-day stories).

\*  \*\*Hill argues for a better approach involving many more much smaller changes.\*\* He contends that the trade is deeply invested in the idea that more value comes faster from fewer and larger steps, but \*\*"that's not true".\*\*

\*  The theory of taking fewer and larger steps has \*\*two significant problems\*\*:
  \*  \*\*It does not take into account non-linearity.\*\* Longer steps don't just get bigger in effort linearly; they get "bigger bigger" due to factors like decreased certainty, reduced human mental bandwidth, and increased perturbation.
  \*  \*\*It does not take into account the inherent value in each step.\*\* This value is realized multiple times with smaller steps, whereas it's only captured once in a large step.

\*  \*\*There are three aspects to the inherent value of smaller steps:\*\*
  \*  \*\*Interruptability:\*\* Being in a "ready" state more frequently allows for easier interruption and resumption of work without significant loss of context.
  \*  \*\*Steerability:\*\* Frequent "ready" states enable better responsiveness to changing priorities, market situations, or urgent issues ("brushfires").
  \*  \*\*"Juice" (Dopamine):\*\* Completing smaller steps more often provides regular positive reinforcement and a sense of accomplishment.

\*  When faced with a big story, the first step is to \*\*"turn the ton of fan out of it"\*\* by removing unnecessary features or functionalities that are not immediately required for the core value. It's also crucial to shift the mindset from requiring improvement with every step to simply ensuring the step doesn't make the user's life worse.

\*  Articulating a clear definition of "done" can help in breaking down stories into smaller, manageable increments.

\*  Techniques like releasing new, unused code (e.g., CSS classes or non-accessible code) immediately can further enable smaller, more frequent deployments. This aligns with the principle of \*\*"open for extension closed for modification"\*\*.

\*  The prevailing pedagogy that focuses on greenfield development is misaligned with the reality that most developers work in brownfield environments, emphasizing the need for better teaching of techniques for changing existing code. Keeping methods ridiculously short (e.g., no more than five lines) can naturally lead to smaller, more manageable changes.

\*  The presenter advocates for a \*\*"human local oriented taken and iterative"\*\* approach to change, applicable not just to code but also to individuals, teams, and organizations.

\*  The core message is summarized by the analogies: \*\*"that's too much rice" and "mmm that's not enough garlic"\*\* for software development, which translates to "you should probably take many more much smaller steps then you're taking now".

\*  Overcoming resistance to taking smaller steps often involves \*\*creating experiences\*\* that demonstrate the benefits, rather than relying solely on arguments.

title: Integrated Tests Are A Scam
description: Integrated tests are a scam. You’re probably writing 2-5% of the integrated tests you need to test thoroughly. You’re probably duplicating unit tests all over the place. Your integrated tests probably duplicate each other all over the place. When an integrated test fails, who knows what’s broken? Integrated tests probably do you more harm than good. Learn the two-pronged attack that solves the problem: collaboration tests and contract tests.

title: Technical leadership and glue work
description: Let's talk about how to allocate glue work deliberately, frame it usefully and make sure that everyone is choosing a career path they actually want to be on.
notes:
"Glue work" is essential technical leadership that ensures project success, yet it can be career-limiting if it replaces the quantifiable technical achievements required for promotion. To protect your career, you must deliberately balance these high-impact tasks with core technical work while ensuring your contributions are visible and aligned with how your organization evaluates success.

* **Definition of Glue Work:** Glue work refers to the essential but often invisible tasks—such as unblocking teammates, reviewing designs for gaps, onboarding new members, and improving communication—that ensure a software project's success.
* **The Recognition Gap:** While glue work is critical for shipping products, it is frequently undervalued or dismissed as "soft skills" rather than technical leadership, leading to engineers being passed over for promotion in favor of those with high "quantifiable" output like code.
* **Career Risk for Juniors:** Performing excessive glue work early in a career is risky. Because performance reviews often focus on individual technical contributions (coding), junior engineers who act as "glue" may fail to meet the specific technical bars required for senior roles.
* **Systemic Gender Bias:** Research indicates a significant imbalance in who performs this work; women are asked to do and volunteer for non-promotable "thankless" tasks (like organizing off-sites or taking notes) significantly more often than men.
* **Skill Development and Stagnation:** Technical skills are not fixed; they are developed through practice. Over-indexing on glue work can lead to "skill rot" in core areas like coding, making it harder to pass technical interviews or be perceived as a senior individual contributor later.
* **Actionable vs. Vague Feedback:** The feedback that an engineer is "not technical enough" is often non-actionable gatekeeping. Effective feedback should be specific, such as requiring a deeper understanding of distributed systems or specific architecture trade-offs.
* **Strategic Visibility:** To be recognized for glue work, it must be framed as a leadership choice driven by technical judgment. Engineers should create artifacts, such as design proposals or strategic documentation, to make their impact visible and undeniable.
* **The "Stop Doing Glue" Strategy:** If an organization refuses to reward glue work despite its impact, the most effective short-term career move is to temporarily stop doing it and focus exclusively on high-visibility, "unarguably technical" tasks to secure a promotion.
* **Managerial Responsibility:** Managers must ensure that non-promotable work is distributed fairly across the entire team and that senior-level career ladders explicitly require the technical leadership skills demonstrated through glue work.
* **Long-term Career Pathing:** Moving from an "engineer" title to a non-technical role (like project management) can close doors, as industry bias often assumes technical skills disappear once the "engineer" title is removed from a resume.

title: How to Deliver Quality Software Against All Odds GOTO 2024
description: This podcast features Daniel Terhorst-North, a prominent figure in the software development world, reflecting on 20 years of industry changes and sharing his insights on topics ranging from Agile and DevOps to product management and organizational flow. Drawing on his experiences at Thoughtworks and beyond, Terhorst-North highlights the importance of connecting business needs with technical implementation and emphasizes the value of building evolvable systems with "simplexity" in mind.
notes:
Effective software delivery is a **socio-technical endeavor** that requires balancing technical excellence—such as continuous delivery and domain-driven design—with strong product management to ensure you are "baking the right cake" for the business. You should remember that the most impactful improvements in engineering come from **aligning code with the language of the domain** and fostering a human-centered culture where communication and organizational flow take priority over technical complexity.

*   **Agile Normalization:** Agile practices have become so ubiquitous over the last 20 years that they are now the industry standard, moving from a "maverick" approach to a normalized, everyday part of software delivery.
*   **Cross-Functional Teams:** The industry has shifted away from siloed gated processes toward cross-functional teams that handle a "full-ish stack" to deliver capabilities rather than just isolated components.
*   **Economic Impact of Architecture:** Efficient code and "green software" are economic imperatives; well-architected systems reduce server resources, electricity consumption, and operational costs, especially in cloud environments.
*   **Serverless Paradigm:** Serverless architecture should be viewed as "shared nothing" rather than strictly stateless. Optimizing the startup and shared components of functions can enable massive scaling at extremely low costs.
*   **Go-Style Testing:** Unlike the traditional xUnit school that stops at the first failure, Go's testing allows for a "roadworthiness" approach where multiple failures are registered in a single run, providing a comprehensive list of what needs fixing.
*   **Domain-Driven Design (DDD):** Incorporating the language of the business domain directly into the code makes complex business rules explicit and allows developers and stakeholders to communicate without needing a translator.
*   **Innovation through Necessity:** Many industry standards, such as CI/CD pipelines, blue-green deployments, and single deployable artifacts, were invented to solve high-pressure, "impossible" delivery challenges in real-world projects.
*   **Theory of Constraints:** Bottlenecks in software delivery often shift: once development becomes agile, the bottleneck moves to operations (release), and once operations is automated, it moves upstream to product management (idea batching).
*   **Product vs. Project Funding:** Project-based funding focuses on cost and perceived risk, while product-based funding acts like a venture investment, prioritizing continuous value delivery and reducing "value at risk."
*   **Socio-Technical Systems:** Programming is a socio-technical activity that is as much about human communication and team energy as it is about technology; technical excellence alone is insufficient for long-term organizational success.
*   **Baking the Right Cake:** High-performing organizations must balance engineering excellence ("baking the cake right") with effective product strategy ("baking the right cake") to avoid efficiently building the wrong thing.
*   **Redefining the "Best" Programmer:** Mastery in programming is not strictly tied to formal education; a computer science degree is neither a requirement for being a top-tier programmer nor a guarantee of expertise.

title: Make Impacts Not Software
description: An essential talk to understand how to get the most impact with the least amount of software (and thereby reduce basal cost and time to market). Highly recommended.
notes:
Success lies in **redefining it from merely delivering software functionalities to achieving measurable business impacts and changes in user behavior**. This entails embracing a dynamic, GPS-like planning method that prioritizes continuous learning, testing assumptions, and adapting to reach the desired destination with the least amount of software possible.

- **Redefine Success as Impact, Not Software:** Shift focus from merely delivering software features or completing user stories to achieving measurable business outcomes (e.g., making/saving money, acquiring customers) and observable changes in user behavior.
- **Embrace Dynamic, GPS-like Planning:** Adopt flexible planning that allows for continuous learning, adaptation, and recalculation of the path, rather than rigid, upfront linear roadmaps.
- **Optimize Delivery for Learning:** Recognize that true understanding of what needs to be delivered emerges through the delivery process itself, requiring plans that anticipate and incorporate learning and change.
- **Focus on Measurable Behavior Changes:** Define project goals in terms of how user or customer behavior will change, making success objectively observable and testable.
- **Unpack and Test Assumptions:** View all software scope as based on assumptions. Visualize these assumptions and actively design experiments to test them quickly and cheaply.
- **Generate Multiple Options:** Create and evaluate several potential solutions or approaches for achieving a desired impact, rather than committing to a single upfront plan.
- **Ask Strategic Questions:** Utilize structured conversations, starting with "Why?" (the ultimate business goal), then "Who?" (the affected users), "How?" (their behavior change), and "What?" (the software to enable that change), to align on objectives.
- **Prioritize Small, Testable Steps:** Break down large goals into small, measurable "next turns" that can be quickly implemented and evaluated to validate the chosen path and assumptions.

title: Continuous Delivery and the Theory of Constraints
description: In this talk, Steve Smith will explain how easy it is for a Continuous Delivery programme to be unsuccessful, how the Theory Of Constraints works, how to apply the Five Focussing Steps to Continuous Delivery, and how to home in on the constrained activities that are your keys to success. It includes tales of glorious failures and ignominious successes when adopting Continuous Delivery.
notes:
To successfully implement continuous delivery, organizations must use the **Theory of Constraints** to identify and focus exclusively on the **primary bottleneck** in their value stream rather than wasting resources on broad automation or maturity models. By balancing the flow of releases to meet actual **product demand** through targeted, incremental experiments, teams can achieve sustainable throughput and stability with minimal, purposeful changes.

* **Continuous Delivery (CD)** is the process of improving the stability and throughput of production deployments until product demand is met.
* Organizations are **complex adaptive systems**, meaning there is no off-the-shelf "recipe" or package that can solve the specific impediments of a given company.
* The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** is the correct tool for running structured experiments, whereas **maturity models** are ineffective and should be avoided.
* Tracking progress requires a **Deployment Throughput Indicator** to visualize both **lead time** (time from version control to production) and **interval** (time between production releases).
* A state of **"sprinting to a standstill"** occurs when the release interval is shorter than the lead time, leading to unsustainable stress and team burnout.
* The **Theory of Constraints (TOC)** is highly applicable to the technology value stream (build, test, and operations) because it is a **homogeneous workflow** that should be deterministic and invariable.
* A **constraint** is any resource with capacity equal to or less than market demand; its performance limits the throughput of the entire organization.
* Improving a **non-constraint** is a waste of time and money because it does not reduce the queue at the bottleneck or increase total system throughput.
* The **Five Focusing Steps** of TOC provide a structured improvement path:
    1. **Identify** the constraint (the activity taking too long for the target lead time).
    2. **Exploit** the constraint (maximize its efficiency and remove waste without initial spending).
    3. **Subordinate** everything else (limit the flow of work to the speed of the constraint).
    4. **Elevate** the constraint (invest money or resources to increase its capacity).
    5. **Repeat** the process to find the next emerging constraint.
* **Target lead time** should be established by determining actual product demand, typically by asking the product owner for the desired frequency of production deployments.
* **Automation** is only beneficial when placed exactly at the constraint; "automating the world" without focus is unlikely to improve time-to-market.
* Success can be achieved **incrementally**; reaching a "good enough" milestone (e.g., fortnightly instead of weekly releases) often satisfies demand while reducing risk and ambiguity.

title: Debt Metaphor
description: Ward Cunningham reflects on the history, motivation and common misunderstanding of the "debt metaphor" as motivation for refactoring.
notes:
**Technical debt arises when software does not reflect a team's current understanding of a problem, leading to "interest" that slows down progress unless the code is continuously refactored to incorporate new learnings. This process requires writing clean code from the beginning so that the program remains flexible enough to be reorganized as insights are gained.**

* The **technical debt metaphor** was created to explain the necessity of **refactoring** by using a financial analogy that non-technical stakeholders can understand.
* **Shipping software early** to gain experience is analogous to **borrowing money**; it allows for immediate progress but creates a "debt" that must eventually be addressed.
* **"Interest"** manifests as the development slowdown that occurs when the code's design no longer aligns with the team's **current understanding** of the problem.
* **Repaying the debt** involves **refactoring** the program to reflect the knowledge and insights gained after the initial release.
* If a program is developed solely by adding features without reorganization, the **"interest" eventually consumes all effort**, leading to a state where **zero progress** is possible.
* Technical debt is **not an excuse for writing poor code**; it is about writing code that reflects a **partial understanding** of a problem as clearly as possible.
* For the metaphor to be effective, the code must be **clean enough to be refactorable** so that debt can actually be "paid back" as the problem domain becomes clearer.
* This concept is a fundamental explanation for the efficacy of **Extreme Programming (XP)** and its focus on continuous improvement and evolutionary design.

title: He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor (Sierra)
description: Legendary builder Bret Taylor offers profound insights on the future of AI agents and their impact on business, the evolving landscape of software development, and critical lessons for product and startup success.
notes:
The talk emphasizes the critical need for **intellectual honesty and relentless self-reflection** to identify the **true, most impactful problems** to solve, rather than defaulting to solutions that align with personal strengths or comfortable assumptions. This involves actively seeking out the actual root causes of issues, even when uncomfortable, and being willing to do what is most impactful for the business, even if it falls outside one's preferred skill set.

**AI and the Future of Software**
- The entire AI market is moving towards **agents and outcomes-based pricing**.
- **Agents are the new app**, becoming the primary product form factor and driving true productivity gains by autonomously accomplishing jobs.
- The act of creating software is transforming from typing code to **operating code-generating machines**.
- **Computer science remains extremely valuable for developing systems thinking**, essential for operating AI code generators and connecting technology with business problems.
- There's a need for a **new programming system designed for AI**, prioritizing verifiability and human leverage over human ergonomics, enabling the creation of robust and complex systems where coding is "free."
- The AI market segments are:
    - **Frontier/Foundation Models:** Dominated by large hyperscalers due to high CapEx; **startups should generally avoid this space**.
    - **Tooling:** Risky due to potential competition from large infrastructure providers moving up the stack.
    - **Applied AI (Agents):** The most significant opportunity for startups, akin to the SaaS market, focusing on building agents for specific business outcomes.
- AI models can serve as **highly effective personalized educational tools**, democratizing access to learning by adapting to individual styles.
- To achieve productivity gains with AI tools, **focus on root cause analysis for incorrect AI outputs and implement AI supervising AI (self-reflection)** to improve robustness.
- AI agents can automate a significant portion of **customer service interactions (50-90%) with high customer satisfaction**.
**Product Strategy**
- The most important lesson in product is to **create entirely new experiences native to the platform**, rather than just digitizing existing solutions or copying competitors.
- Successful products must answer the fundamental question: "**Why should a customer use this thing in the first place?**"
- Product design should focus on **systems that deliver delightful experiences even with messy, uncontrolled real-world data inputs**.
- Adding "sizzle" (like Google Maps' satellite imagery) can generate **viral moments and initial adoption**, complementing the product's core enduring value.
**Business Models & Go-to-Market**
- **Outcome-based pricing is the superior model for AI products**, especially when AI autonomously performs a job with measurable results, aligning vendor and customer incentives.
- **Usage-based pricing (e.g., per token) is often a poor indicator of value** for AI products, as it doesn't necessarily correlate with actual business outcomes.
    - **Choosing the correct go-to-market motion is critical:Developer-led:** For platform products where engineers have buying latitude.
    - **Product-led Growth (PLG):** When the user and buyer are the same person.
    - **Direct Sales:** Increasingly relevant for AI products where the buyer and user are different, and **founders should embrace this approach** even if it's outside their comfort zone.
**Mindset & Leadership**
- Consistently ask: "**What is the most impactful thing I could do today?**" and prioritize tasks based on maximizing impact for the business.
- Cultivate **intellectual honesty and relentless self-reflection** to identify the true, most impactful problems, rather than defaulting to solutions that align with personal strengths or assumptions.
- Be **flexible with your professional identity** and willing to adapt to what the company needs from you at any given time.
- **Seek and listen to advice from external advisors and boards who will tell you what you _need_ to hear**, even if it's uncomfortable.
- When receiving advice, **always ask "why"** to understand the underlying framework and avoid applying anecdotal experiences as universal rules.
- **Develop good judgment** by rigorously reflecting on past decisions and understanding the context of advice.

title: Building effective engineering teams; lessons from 10 years at Google | Addy Osmani
description: An exploration of how Engineering Culture integrates Technical Leadership, Management, and Developer Productivity to optimize Teams through Agile and DevOps practices.
notes:
Fostering a strong Engineering Culture is the essential foundation for synchronizing Technical Leadership, Management, and Agile practices to optimize Developer Productivity within Teams. Achieving this alignment ensures that Technology Strategy and Technical Practices work together to drive continuous improvement and successful Product delivery.

- **Engineering Culture** serves as the unifying framework for **Technical Leadership**, **Management**, and the development of **Teams**.
- High-performing organizations prioritize **Developer Productivity** and **Developer Experience (Devex)** to maximize organizational **Flow**.
- The core of technical success involves embedding **Agile**, **XP**, and **DevOps** practices to ensure reliable **Continuous Delivery**.
- **Systems Thinking** and **Team Topologies** are essential for designing organizations that can handle **Scalability** and complex **Architecture**.
- **Technical Practices** must include **Evolutionary Design**, **Software Design**, and **Craftsmanship** to effectively manage **Legacy Code**.
- Success requires aligning **Technology Strategy** with **Product Strategy** through **Lean Startup** and **Product Discovery** principles.
- Continuous improvement is driven by a focus on **Feedback Cycles**, **Testing**, **Refactoring**, and **Observability**.

title: Andrew Ng: Building Faster with AI
description: Andrew Ng's talk provides best practices for startups to achieve unprecedented execution speed by leveraging new AI technology for rapid engineering, product iteration, and strategic decision-making
notes:
A core takeaway from Andrew Ng's talk is that **unprecedented execution speed is paramount for startup success**, a velocity now uniquely enabled by **new AI technology that drastically accelerates engineering and prototyping**. This rapid development, in turn, makes **swift user feedback and precise product iteration** the critical new bottleneck, underscoring the necessity of understanding AI to quickly build products users genuinely love.

- **Execution speed is paramount for startup success**, a velocity now uniquely enabled by **new AI technology that drastically accelerates engineering and prototyping**.
- The **greatest opportunities for startups lie at the application layer** of the AI stack, particularly leveraging the rise of **agentic AI** and its orchestration layer.
- To achieve speed, **focus on concrete, actionable product ideas** that engineers can quickly build and test, avoiding vague concepts.
- **AI coding assistance enables rapid engineering, making prototype development 10x or more faster** and significantly reducing engineering costs, thereby transforming decision-making from "one-way doors" to "two-way doors."
- With engineering accelerating, **product management—specifically getting user feedback and deciding what features to build—becomes the critical new bottleneck**.
- Employ a **portfolio of tactics for obtaining rapid product feedback**, prioritizing quick, iterative cycles (from expert intuition to user testing with strangers) over slower methods like AB testing, and use this data to hone instincts.
- **Deeply understanding AI and its various building blocks** (e.g., prompting, fine-tuning, RAG, evals, guardrails) provides a substantial advantage, enabling teams to combine elements to create novel software.
- **Empower everyone in all job roles to learn to code or effectively steer AI**, as this drastically enhances productivity and commands greater influence over computer outputs.
- The **singular, most important focus for any business is building a product that users genuinely love**, as this foundation outweighs concerns about moats or other factors in early stages.
- Be aware of **AI hype and dangerous narratives**, recognizing that AI's "safety" is a function of its responsible application rather than the technology itself, and resist efforts to centralize control or shut down open-source AI.

title: Digital Transformation
description: A good summary of how accelerating the iteration pace is fundamental for innovation and how the cloud can help with it.
notes:
Successful digital transformation requires organizations to unblock innovation by shifting from centralized, slow decision-making to a decentralized model that prioritizes speed of delivery and automated, cloud-native architectures. By fostering a culture of continuous learning and empowering product-focused teams to "run what they wrote," companies can effectively scale their operations and respond immediately to evolving customer demands.

* **Digital Transformation Shift**: Transitioning from "Old World IT"—characterized by managing physical assets, intermediaries, and print/TV media—to a "New World" of direct, real-time customer connections via mobile apps, IoT, and continuous tracking.
* **Direct Customer Relationship**: Moving beyond retailers and intermediaries to connect directly with end-users, enabling extreme personalization and the use of massive customer analytics data.
* **The Four Innovation Blockers**:
    * **Culture**: Centralized decision-making and low trust hinder speed; organizations must shift to localized, fast decision-making and flexible processes.
    * **Skills**: Cloud technology is often just "unfamiliar" rather than difficult; training existing staff through small "Pathfinder" teams is more effective than trying to hire externally [4-6].
    * **Organization**: Shifting from short-term projects to long-term product teams that follow the "run what you wrote" (DevOps) principle to reduce technical debt and increase iteration speed.
    * **Finance**: Managing the shift from Capital Expenditure (CapEx) to Operational Expenditure (OpEx) by signaling the change to the CFO and the market to avoid architectural slowing.
* **The Transformation Pathway**:
    * **Speed**: Initially optimizing for "time to value"—reducing the window from code commit to customer reach from months to hours.
    * **Scale**: Once speed is established, optimizing for horizontal scaling, high capacity utilization, and cost efficiency.
    * **Strategic**: Finally moving business-critical, high-value, or safety-critical workloads to the cloud and pursuing full data center replacement.
* **Cloud Native Principles**: Embracing a model that is pay-as-you-go, self-service, API-driven, globally distributed by default, and uses immutable code deployments (containers or AMIs).
* **Resilience and Chaos Engineering**: Recognizing that failure is inevitable and focusing on fast detection and response; using "Chaos Engineering" as a proactive fire drill to ensure staff and systems can automatically route around issues.

title: Product Tank Madrid: Focus on Impact with John Cutler - World Product Day 2020
description: Excellent talk. John presents all the learnings he had during the last three years, using his great article as a starting point (<https://cutle.fish/blog/12-signs-youre-working-in-a-feature-factory>). Lots of knowledge and tips to introduce product thinking.
notes:
True impact in product development is achieved by shifting from a "feature factory" mindset of constant output to a culture of outcomes, where teams prioritize measuring for learning and demonstrating value through small, visible experiments. Success depends on reducing work-in-progress to create space for discovery and establishing a shared language of strategy that connects product behaviors directly to the broader goals of the business.

*   **Show, Don't Tell:** Most executives and team members have never seen a high-performing product team in action. To build belief, you must demonstrate success through small, rapid experiments rather than trying to convince others through theory or slides.
*   **Measure to Learn vs. Measure to Control:** There is a critical distinction between using metrics to grade, rank, or justify decisions and using them to reduce uncertainty. Focus on building a culture where measurement is a tool for improvement and collaborative learning.
*   **Side-by-Side Practice:** Impact-focused product development is a skill that requires hands-on practice. Experienced leaders should work directly alongside teams to model how to ask better questions and shape work, rather than deciding strategy in a silo.
*   **Manage Work-in-Progress (WIP):** High WIP is a primary driver of the "Feature Factory" symptoms. When teams are overloaded, they lose the patience and capacity to experiment. Visualizing work at the "mission" or "outcome" level is necessary to see how over-commitment impacts throughput and morale.
*   **Strategy as a Shared Language:** A product strategy is not a roadmap; it is a shared language around value. It must provide the connective tissue between specific customer behaviors (outcomes) and broader business goals like revenue or churn.
*   **Explain the "So What":** You must evangelize the business value of working in an outcome-oriented way. Since shipping a pre-agreed roadmap is often perceived as the "safest" path in a corporate environment, you must demonstrate why focusing on impact leads to more sustainable growth.
*   **Acknowledge and Unlearn:** Many companies become "Feature Factories" because that high-output approach initially made them successful. Moving toward impact requires acknowledging that past success while "unlearning" structures and incentives that no longer work as the product grows in complexity.
*   **Visualize the Mission:** To shift focus, move beyond tracking individual tasks and start visualizing the progress of higher-level missions and bets. This helps the entire organization understand the data and the purpose behind the work.

title: Shared Mental Models Part 1
description: This was an excellent talk, full of insights that prompted reflection. In this talk Jessica looks at how the shared mental models created while mob programming work throughout the team even when they are not actively mobbing. She also explores the other practices she’s found complementary in creating a high functioning team and how looking at your contribution from a generative (helping other create) vs a productive (what I created, myself) frame can lead to a happier, better and more productive team.
notes:
True excellence in software engineering is achieved when we shift our focus from individual productivity to **generativity**, recognizing that great teams synthesize great people through shared mental models and collaborative learning. You should remember that we function within a **symmathesy**—a learning system where the team and the software evolve together, meaning your growth as a developer is a direct product of the healthy, communicative environment you help create.

* **Great teams make great people.** Individual greatness is not the starting point but a side effect of being part of a high-functioning system. Great developers are "synthesized" by their environment and team.
* **Symmathesy** is a "learning system made of learning parts." In software, this is a **socio-technical symmathesy** where the people, the code, the running software, and the tools all learn and change together over time.
* **The Line of Representation** is the boundary between developers and the software they create. Because digital systems are not physical, developers must consciously build and maintain tools (logs, monitors, editors) to discover what the software is doing and build accurate mental models.
* **The Law of Downhill Invention and Uphill Analysis** states that it is significantly easier to build a complex system from scratch (downhill) than it is to understand or change a system built by someone else (uphill).
* **Legacy code** is defined as code that a developer can no longer "change" because they no longer hold the mental model of it in their head. It is no longer a "living" part of the symmathesy.
* **Mental models are always incomplete.** In complex systems, individual mental models are inevitably out of date or wrong. A team's primary task is spending the "coherence penalty" to align these overlapping models so they can communicate and act effectively.
* **Generativity over Productivity.** Rather than measuring individual output, team members should be evaluated on their "generativity"—the difference in what the team produces and what the team *becomes* because of that person's presence.
* **The "10x Developer" can have negative generativity.** A developer who works alone and changes the system faster than others can learn it prevents the rest of the team from building a mental model, effectively creating legacy code in their wake.
* **Mob Programming** is a vital practice for sharing mental models. It ensures that the system remains "alive" in the heads of multiple people simultaneously, allowing the team to move "downhill" together.
* **The "Invisible College" effect.** Innovation and creativity are rarely the result of isolated individuals; they emerge from groups of diverse people (a "Camerata") sharing common values, language, and problems.

title: DevOps for the Database
Making Work Visible: How to Unmask Capacity Killing WIP
description: Are you scrambling to meet deadlines only to find more requests piling up in your inbox? Hidden WIP is a major contributor to clogged value streams and overloaded employees, burdened with too many meetings and frustrating work routines. Making work visible highlights the problems leading to low productivity across the organization. In this talk, Dominica shares how to unmask the things that are killing your team’s capacity and their ability to optimize workflow.
notes:
To effectively manage work overload and consistently deliver business value, it is crucial to make all work, including unplanned tasks and dependencies, explicitly visible and to intentionally limit work-in-progress (WIP) to optimize workflow. By doing so, teams can identify bottlenecks, address conflicting priorities, and ensure a smoother flow of activities from start to finish.

- People often take on more work than they can capacity to do, leading to self and team overload, bottlenecks, and delayed business value delivery.
- Reasons for overload include a desire not to let the team down, the appeal of new projects over finishing old ones, underestimating task duration, and difficulty saying no, especially to superiors.
- **Making all work visible** is crucial to unmask capacity-killing overload, including planned, unplanned, and partially completed tasks.
- **Too much Work-In-Progress (WIP)** is the primary deterrent to smooth workflow (FLOW), causing scattered focus, interruptions, context switching, and significantly increasing wait times.
- Visualize and plan for **unplanned work** (emergencies, interruptions) by allocating capacity or creating dedicated visibility lanes, as it will never be eliminated entirely.
- Address **conflicting priorities** by establishing clear prioritization policies and using visual boards to show competing demands, facilitating conversations about what truly is the top priority.
- Expose **dependencies** between tasks, teams, or systems, as these significantly increase the likelihood of delays and contribute to WIP overload.
- **Intentionally limit Work-In-Progress (WIP)** to create a pull system, which gives teams permission to say no to new tasks when capacity is full, ensuring a smoother flow and more predictable delivery of business value.
- Gradually reduce WIP limits over time to find an optimal flow, adjusting them as team capacity changes.
- Leaders must be aware of their positional power, as team members are likely to accept new work even when overloaded, which ultimately hinders the delivery of business value.

title: Velocity and Volume or Speed Wins
description: A classic talk to understand how to leverage the cloud, continuous delivery and devops to create modern web-scale systems.
notes:
To remain competitive at modern scale, organizations must prioritize **velocity and continuous delivery** by shifting to a "cloud-native" mindset that assumes system components will inevitably fail and builds **anti-fragile architectures** to handle that reality [1-4]. Success depends on **empowering developers** through self-service automation and shortening feedback loops to iterate and innovate faster than any rival

* **Digital Transformation Scale:** Over the last 20 years, companies have shifted from managing thousands of entities (like POS terminals) to millions of web transactions and customers, representing a three-orders-of-magnitude increase in operational complexity.
* **Mindset Shift:** The traditional mainframe mindset of relying on hardware that doesn't fail and spending a year making software "stable" is no longer valid in a cloud-based, web-scale world.
* **Fragility of Speed and Scale:** At high volume, scale breaks hardware, and continuous speed breaks software; systems must be designed to assume that every component or dependency could be broken at any time.
* **High Availability Architecture:** Reliability is achieved through active-active regional deployments (e.g., East Coast and West Coast simultaneously), triple-replicated data, and DNS routing to abstract away infrastructure failures [6-8].
* **Game Day Practices:** Organizations must conduct "fire drills" or game day practices to ensure teams know how to respond to incidents; otherwise, highly reliable systems become dangerous because no one knows what to do when they eventually fail.
* **Developer Self-Service:** Continuous deployment removes the handoff between developers and IT; developers are empowered to put code into production themselves but must also be responsible for its operation.
* **The Benefit of "Peril":** Putting developers on pager duty for their own code creates a sense of "peril" that aligns their behavior with production stability, leading to more reliable code and faster incident resolution.
* **DevOps Evolution:** Modern DevOps involves developers learning to perform operations (Dev-focused) or operations teams learning to automate through development.
* **GitHub as the New Vendor:** Traditional vendor evaluation and customization cycles have been replaced by consuming and contributing to open-source projects on GitHub.
* **Accelerated OODA Loop:** Success depends on the speed of the feedback loop (Observe, Orient, Decide, Act). Competitive advantage is won by moving through this cycle in hours or minutes rather than months or years [16-19].
* **Decision Empowerment:** Innovation is hindered by permission-based cultures; high-velocity companies shift from "asking for permission" to "sharing decisions".
* **Big Data for Hypotheses:** Big Data should be used to quickly answer unasked questions about customer pain points, allowing for hypothesis-driven development and rapid AB testing.
* **Automated Deployment Pipeline:** A modern flow involves automated builds, baking machine images (AMIs), functional/performance testing, and canary analysis before reaching production.
* **Canary and Red-Black Pushes:** Production safety is managed through canary tests (comparing new code side-by-side with old code using statistical signature analysis) and red-black deployments that allow for instant rollbacks.
* **Global Rollout Strategy:** To minimize risk, code should be deployed first to smaller, low-traffic regions during their off-peak hours (e.g., Europe at night for a US company) before rolling out to major regions.
* **Anti-fragility:** Systems should be intentionally stressed to make them stronger; like exercise, controlled stress prevents catastrophic failure during unexpected events.
* **Professional Identity:** GitHub has become the primary resume for developers and the "app store" for companies, where the quality of public code is more important than whiteboard interviews.

title: Who Does What by How Much? Customer-Centric OKRs explained with Jeff Gothelf and Josh Seiden #ccokrs
description: Experts Jeff Gothelf and Josh Seiden detail how to define customer-centric Objectives and Key Results (OKRs) by focusing on measurable outcomes, encapsulated in the framework’s core question: "Who Does What By How Much?"
notes:
Effective goal setting using Objectives and Key Results (OKRs) must shift focus away from managing outputs (making a specific "thing") towards measuring measurable changes in **human behavior**—defined by the question, **"Who Does What By How Much?"**—which are the true indicators of delivered value and strategic success. This outcome-centric approach encourages organizations to **embrace agility** by constantly testing assumptions and changing course based on evidence, rather than relying on risky, long-term predictions about what to build.

- **Outcome-Centric Goal Setting:** The most important shift in goal setting is moving from managing outputs (making a specific "thing") to managing outcomes, which are measurable changes in human behavior that deliver value.
- **The Core Question for Outcomes:** The definition of an outcome is encapsulated in the question: "**Who Does What By How Much?**" This framework ensures goals are customer-centric, focused on a specific human, their changed behavior, and the degree of that change.
- **OKRs as a Strategy Expression:** Objectives and Key Results (OKRs) must be an expression of your organization's strategy—the opinionated approach to solving its most important problems and succeeding in the market. Implementing OKRs without considering strategy makes the framework a mechanical exercise.
- **Objectives (O) Definition:** Objectives are qualitative, aspirational, inspirational, and time-bound goals that describe the desired future state of the world, focusing on solving a problem (e.g., "the simplest," "the safest"). Objectives should explicitly exclude features, solutions, or metrics.
- **Key Results (KR) Definition:** Key Results are the measurable metrics that answer: "How will we know we have achieved the objective?" They must be outcomes (measurable changes in human behavior) and are ideally expressed as ratios or rates (e.g., 25% more of this). Key Results should also exclude features or solutions.
- **Embracing Uncertainty:** Organizations must acknowledge that they are constantly guessing because the future is unpredictable, especially with digital products. Modern technology and methodologies like Agile and Lean UX reduce the risk of guessing incorrectly, but organizations must use outcomes to course-correct quickly based on evidence.
- **The Customer is Always Human:** Every person in an organization has a customer—the human who consumes the product, system, or service they create. This includes external consumers, vendors, or internal colleagues. Measuring success requires measuring the behavior change of this specific human customer.
- **Iterating on OKRs:** OKRs are based on assumptions and should not be static. Teams must use a regular cycle (commonly quarterly) to pause, reflect, and iterate on their objectives and key results based on new evidence and learning.
- **Avoiding the "Output Trap" (The Anti-Pattern):** Managing to output carries high risk because it assumes certainty about what to build. While managing output may make sense for high-certainty, low-risk endeavors (like building a bridge), it is a dangerous anti-pattern in modern product development.
- **Internal OKRs for Support Teams:** It is possible for internal teams (like IT or platform teams) to set valid OKRs by defining internal colleagues as their customers and measuring the value delivered to them (e.g., reducing deployment time from X to Y for developer teams).
- **OKRs and Agility:** OKRs naturally integrate with Agile methodologies like Scrum, where sprints can be used for discovery activities and testing assumptions to see if they are moving the Key Results forward, thereby encouraging genuine agility by changing course based on evidence.

title: Five Cloud Native Ops Superpowers: Yes, You Can Do That!
Cultivating Instinct
The future of software engineering
description: Interesting journey through the history of our profession and some predictions about the future with one of the main protagonists (Grady Booch). Very interesting, both the talk and the subsequent questions.
notes:
Software engineering is a noble and rapidly evolving discipline characterized by rising levels of abstraction, where every technical choice carries a profound ethical responsibility to shape the world intentionally for the better [1-4]. Success in the field's future depends on mastering fundamental architectural principles—such as crisp abstractions and continuous delivery—while remaining vigilant about the moral and social consequences of the code we write.

* **Software engineering is a young profession** characterized by a continuous rise in levels of abstraction and the resolution of competing forces such as functionality, reliability, cost, schedule, and ethics.
* **Software architecture consists of the most significant design decisions**, where significance is specifically measured by the cost of change.
* **Successful software projects require a regular rhythm or "heartbeat" of releases**, typically enabled by a robust CI/CD pipeline.
* **The development process is a series of overlapping rhythms** involving discovery, invention, and implementation rather than a strictly linear path.
* **The evolution of software engineering has been driven by shifting economics**, moving from a time when machines were more expensive than humans to the present day where human time is the primary cost.
* **AI represents a shift from "programming" to "teaching" systems**, defining artificial intelligence as systems that can both reason (inductive, abductive, or causal) and learn.
* **Modern AI is currently dominated by inductive pattern matching** (neural networks), but the future likely lies in neuro-symbolic architectures that combine learning with symbolic reasoning.
* **AI development is adopting classic software engineering practices**, including design patterns (like the Transformer architecture), version control, and continuous integration.
* **Every line of code represents an ethical and moral decision**, as software increasingly impacts the physical world and human rights.
* **Fundamental engineering principles remain constant** regardless of technology: crisp abstractions, clear separation of concerns, balanced distribution of responsibilities, and simplicity.
* **Distributed and remote development are permanent** shifts in the engineering landscape, proven resilient by the global response to the pandemic.
* **Architectural decisions should be delayed as long as possible** to minimize the cost of change, but eventually, they must be made to provide the system with necessary form and function.
* **Digital currencies are considered inevitable and desirable**, though current implementations like private cryptocurrencies and NFTs face significant hurdles regarding regulation, accountability, and environmental impact.

title: YOW! 2019 Evolutionary Design Animated (Part1)
description: Modern software development welcomes changing requirements, even late in the process, but how can we write our software so that those changes don’t create a mess? Evolutionary design is the key. It’s a technique that emerges from Extreme Programming, the method that brought us test-driven development, merciless refactoring, and continuous integration. James Shore first encountered Extreme Programming and evolutionary design nearly 20 years ago. Initially skeptical, he’s explored its boundaries ever since. In this session, James will share what he’s learned through in-depth animations of real software projects. You’ll see how designs evolve over time and you’ll learn how and when to use evolutionary design for your own projects. Part 2: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wDZCF8jfeMc
notes:
The presentation by James Shore is about evolutionary design, which is a way to achieve the enabling practice principle of modern software development. Here are the key points:
- **Evolutionary design starts with the simplest possible design and grows it alongside the application’s needs**. It comes out of extreme programming (XP), which was the first popular agile method and set the tone for a lot of what is taken for granted in agile today. XP brought ideas like test-driven development, user stories, and continuous integration.
- **XP is unique because it provides the technical practices to make agile possible, such as test-driven development and continuous integration**.
- A core premise of XP is inverting the cost of change so that changes are not more expensive later in the development process.
- **Evolutionary design depends on refactoring, which is changing the design of code without changing its behavior**. It is not appropriate when refactoring is not possible or very expensive.
- Evolutionary design may not be appropriate when publishing interfaces to other teams or when multiple teams own the same code base, as a high level of coordination is required.
- Evolutionary design has three pillars:
    - **Simple Design:** Only build what is needed right now. Start with a walking skeleton and do the simplest thing that could possibly work. Add one feature at a time, only adding things when there is a proven need.
    - **Continuous Design:** Constantly review and improve the design. Practices from extreme programming, such as merciless refactoring, collective ownership, pair programming, mob programming, and continuous integration, help make this work.
    - **Reflective Design:** Make design decisions based on the code that exists, not on speculation about what might be useful in the future. Identify flaws, reverse engineer the design if necessary, imagine how to improve the design, and refactor the code to incrementally reach the new design idea.
- **Breakthroughs are interesting things that happen with continuous, merciless refactoring**. Eric Evans calls it a breakthrough whenever a little refactoring makes progress in the design.
    - **Small Scale**: If this is done long enough, new ideas will become visible, resulting in a breakthrough where there is an idea for a dramatic way to improve the design. This can happen at a really small scale or a large scale. Inside of a single module, this kind of breakthrough can happen every hour or two.
    - **Larger Scale**: Larger scale breakthroughs can also occur across multiple classes or modules, typically multiple times per week. Breakthroughs that are more substantial and involve the larger application architecture can happen a few times a year.
    - An example of a small-scale breakthrough occurred in the test-driven development exercise, where the common parsing method was factored out. A larger scale breakthrough example can be found in the presenter's content management engine when he was implementing support for Jade and realized that he needed a better way of handling static files and Jade files without spaghetti code, so it was extracted out into new modules.
- **Simple design is not sloppy design**. For any design decision, ask how hard it will be to change that decision.
- **The presenter offers seven rules for simple design, all of which are derived from other sources**:
    - Every concept should be represented in the application once.
    - Make sure the design intent is clear and obvious.
    - Make the code concrete, not speculative.
    - The code should be cohesive.
    - If a piece of code cannot be seen, it should not be affected by the work being done.
    - Anything that is used in a lot of places should have some sort of abstraction that can be used to change the implementation without changing the way it is used.
    - Isolate changes, especially from third-party libraries.
- When deciding whether to buy or build, consider the cost of maintaining the software that is bought. The presenter's personal rule of thumb is that if something will take less than a week to build, they will seriously consider building it themselves. If it will take less than a day, they will generally build it themselves.
- **With reflective design, focus efforts on where they will do the most good**. It is okay to stop because the issues will be caught the next time.
- With evolutionary design, everything is possible as long as the design is kept clean.
- **Be patient because the best design is rarely known when first working on something**. Take a best shot and let it go, knowing that it can be improved next time.
- **Reducing the cost of change is critical. If doing Agile development where requirements are taken late, evolutionary design is needed**.

title: Architecture for Flow with Wardley Mapping, DDD, and Team Topologies
description: This talk illustrates the concepts, connects the dots between DDD, Wardley mapping and team topologies, and demonstrates how these techniques help to evolve a fictitious legacy system for a fast flow of change.
notes:
Achieving a fast flow of change requires a holistic integration of business strategy, software architecture, and team organization through the combined use of Wardley Mapping, Domain-Driven Design, and Team Topologies. This alignment ensures that the entire socio-technical system is optimized for overall performance and adaptability rather than just the efficiency of its individual parts.

**Holistic Framework**: Integrate Wardley Mapping (Strategy), Domain-Driven Design (Software Design), and Team Topologies (Organization Design) to create adaptive socio-technical systems optimized for a fast flow of change.

**Systems Thinking**: Focus on improving the performance of the system as a whole rather than local optimization of separate parts. A system's performance is determined by how its parts interact, not how they perform individually.

**Wardley Mapping for Situational Awareness**: Use maps to visualize the value chain and the evolution of components (Genesis, Custom Build, Product/Rental, Commodity). This helps determine where to build in-house, where to buy, and how to anticipate market changes.

**DDD Subdomain Strategy**: 
*   **Core Domains**: Provide competitive advantage; build in-house using agile methods.
*   **Supporting Subdomains**: Necessary but provide no edge; use off-the-shelf products or open source where possible.
*   **Generic Subdomains**: Standardized problems (e.g., authentication); outsource to utility suppliers or use commodity services.

**Bounded Contexts as Ownership Boundaries**: Decompose systems into modular bounded contexts that serve as clear ownership boundaries for teams, fostering autonomy and reducing communication overhead.

**Team Cognitive Load**: Optimize for flow by ensuring a team's responsibilities do not exceed its cognitive capacity. Teams can generally manage more components in the "commodity" stage than in the "genesis" stage due to lower uncertainty.

**Four Team Types**:
*   **Stream-aligned**: Focused on a continuous flow of work from a segment of the business domain.
*   **Platform**: Provides internal self-service tools and services to reduce the burden on stream-aligned teams.
*   **Enabling**: Helps stream-aligned teams bridge capability gaps.
*   **Complicated Subsystem**: Handles highly specialized parts of the system (optional).

**Interaction Modes**: Use specific modes—**Collaboration** (joint discovery), **Facilitation** (helping/teaching), and **X-as-a-Service** (predictable delivery)—and evolve these modes as components mature.

**Efficiency Gaps and Bottlenecks**: Identify and eliminate delivery bottlenecks (e.g., manual infrastructure) by moving to commodity services (e.g., cloud/serverless) to free teams to focus on core value.

**Team-First Transition**: Implement architectural changes (like migrating a monolith to microservices) by reorganizing teams first. Start with a platform team to explore new technologies, then incrementally refactor the application into stream-aligned teams.

Nearly all programmers occasionally function as API designers, whether they know it or not. A well-designed API can be a great asset to the organization that wrote it and to all who use it. title: Rethinking growing engineers in the age of AI
description: Meri Williams explores the urgent need to rethink engineering growth in the age of AI, advocating for a shift from manual coding tasks toward early tech leadership, systems thinking, and a "healthy paranoia" that allows leaders to "surf" the waves of technological change rather than be overwhelmed by them.
notes:
Engineering leaders must proactively **redesign training models** to replace the "deliberate practice" once provided by repetitive junior tasks now handled by AI, focusing instead on accelerating **foundational skills, systems thinking, and code review** much earlier in an engineer's career. Ultimately, rather than resisting technological shifts, organizations should embrace a sense of **"healthy paranoia"** and teach their teams to "surf" the waves of AI by prioritizing critical thinking and a deep understanding of why systems work or fail.

- **Disruption of Entry-Level Roles:** AI coding assistants are performing the well-scoped, repetitive tasks traditionally assigned to junior engineers, leading to a significant reduction—estimated at 75%—in entry-level hiring across the industry.
- **Loss of Deliberate Practice:** Traditional junior work provided "accidental" deliberate practice through repetitive tasks and immediate feedback; because AI now handles this work, leaders must find new ways to help engineers build foundational skills like debugging and recognizing "code smells".
- **Accelerated Career Expectations:** Junior engineers are being required to perform "tech lead" functions, such as systems thinking and code review, much earlier in their careers because AI allows them to handle larger stories and complex tasks immediately.
- **New Required Skillsets:** Beyond coding, engineers must now prioritize critical thinking, effective AI prompting, and "healthy paranoia" to verify AI-generated outputs against documentation and real-world behavior.
- **AI as an "Endlessly Patient Mentor":** Junior staff are increasingly using AI to explain code and troubleshoot, which provides constant support but lacks the wisdom of human experience and may reduce traditional coaching interactions.
- **Bridging the "Unconscious Competence" Gap:** Senior engineers often struggle to teach because they perform expert tasks automatically; they must learn to actively deconstruct their knowledge and explain the "why" behind design decisions rather than hiding behind "best practices".
- **Adapting Training Models:** Rather than resisting AI, engineering leaders must redesign training to focus on foundations and mentorship, adopting the philosophy that while you "can't hold back the ocean" of technological change, you can "learn to surf".

title: Improving Software Flow Build Stuff
description: Great presentation, in which Randy, starts from the 5 ideals of the Unicorn project (Locality and Simplicity, Focus, Flow, and Joy, Improvement of Daily Work, Psychological Safety, Customer Focus) to describe what we can do as technical leaders and as engineers to improve our ability to build and deliver software.
notes:
To achieve high-performing software delivery, organizations must cultivate a **virtuous cycle of continuous improvement** and **psychological safety**, where modular architecture and focused flow allow teams to prioritize meaningful customer outcomes over mere technical output. Success depends on the insight that **improving daily work is as vital as the work itself**, requiring a disciplined, blameless approach to problem-solving and a commitment to diverse, inclusive collaboration.

*   **Locality and Simplicity**: Architectural changes should be local and isolated to prevent breaking distant parts of the system. This principle drives the evolution from monoliths to modular microservices, as seen at companies like eBay, Twitter, and Amazon [1-3].
*   **Conway's Law**: A duality should exist between modular architecture and modular organization, where small, autonomous teams manage individual services to allow for independent design, development, and deployment.
*   **Focus as a Predictor of Excellence**: Cognitive control—the ability to focus—is the best predictor of high achievement and individual success. Practices like mindfulness or meditation can help train this attention.
*   **The Flow State**: High performance is achieved when individuals are in a "flow state," where there is a perfect balance between the work challenge and the person's skills.
*   **Maker vs. Manager Schedules**: Developers (makers) require long, uninterrupted blocks of time to maintain flow, while managers often operate on fragmented schedules. Leaders should protect developer time by avoiding meetings scheduled in the middle of these work blocks.
*   **Limiting Work in Progress (WIP)**: Teams should focus on fewer tasks at once and release incremental units of work to get faster feedback and reduce the risk of abandoning large amounts of unfinished work [11-13].
*   **Improvement Budget**: Teams should break the cycle of technical debt by allocating a dedicated budget (often 20–30%) for improving tools, builds, and infrastructure rather than negotiating every small fix.
*   **The Improvement Kata**: Improving daily work is even more important than doing daily work itself. Use the "Plan-Do-Check-Act" cycle to conduct small experiments that iteratively improve the system.
*   **Psychological Safety**: This is the most critical factor for team success; it allows members to take interpersonal risks and bring their "whole selves" to work without fear of punishment or humiliation [19-21].
*   **Blameless Postmortems**: After an incident, teams should focus on systems-level learning—improving detection, diagnosis, and prevention—rather than finding individuals to blame.
*   **Diversity and Decision-Making**: Diverse and inclusive teams make measurably better business decisions and deliver stronger results than homogeneous teams.
*   **Outcomes Over Outputs**: Success should be measured by business results (revenue, profitability, market share) and customer metrics rather than just the volume of code produced [25-27].
*   **Disciplined Problem Solving**: The core job of an engineer is to solve customer problems; sometimes the most efficient solution involves process or assembly line changes rather than writing new code [28-30].

title: Second Generation Lean Product Development Flow
description:
An introduction to Lean Product Development Flow given by Don Reinertsen at Adventures with Agile in London. 
This talk is a must to understand modern product development (Flow, uncertainty, Little's law, cost of delay, the value of feedback, queues, batch size, etc).
notes:
Effective product development requires shifting focus from maximizing resource utilization to managing the **economic flow of information** by quantifying the **cost of delay** and reducing **batch sizes**. To drive innovation, organizations must move beyond traditional manufacturing logic to actively manage **invisible queues** and exploit **variability** through fast feedback loops that allow for timely, evidence-based pivots.

*   **Focus on Economic Models:** Product development involves interacting variables that require quantification to reach system-level optimums. Use life-cycle profit as the primary metric to translate disparate variables (like cycle time and unit cost) into a single unit of measure.
*   **Cost of Delay (CoD):** Quantifying the cost per month of delaying a project is essential. Even rough analytical estimates are superior to intuition, which typically varies by a 50-to-1 range among team members.
*   **Managing Invisible Queues:** Unlike manufacturing, engineering queues consist of invisible information. Overloading capacity causes a nonlinear explosion in queue size and cycle time; for example, reducing capacity by 25% at high utilization can double or triple cycle times.
*   **The Utilization Trap:** Operating at near-100% utilization is efficient for machines but disastrous for flow in processes with variability. Successful product development requires maintaining a capacity margin to manage queues.
*   **Exploiting Variability:** While manufacturing seeks to eliminate variability, product development relies on it for innovation. By using asymmetric payoff functions—similar to financial options—teams can achieve high upside with limited downside.
*   **Batch Size Reduction:** There is a direct relationship between batch size and cycle time. Reducing batch sizes accelerates feedback, improves quality, and reduces risk.
*   **Lowering Transaction Costs:** To economically enable smaller batches, teams must focus on reducing the transaction cost of the process (e.g., automating software testing or deployment).
*   **WIP Constraints:** Starting work sooner does not mean finishing sooner. Controlling Work-In-Progress (WIP) is the most effective way to reduce cycle time, as defined by Little's Law (Cycle Time = WIP / Departure Rate).
*   **Weighted Shortest Job First (WSJF):** Prioritize work by dividing the Cost of Delay by the duration of the task. This ensures the maximum economic benefit is produced per unit of time consumed.
*   **Fast Feedback Loops:** Rapid feedback creates "embedded options" to pivot or shut down failing initiatives early. This improves the overall economics by preventing the waste of resources on low-value features.
*   **Shortened Planning Horizons:** High-speed development improves requirement accuracy. It is easier to define what a customer needs six months from now than three years from now.
*   **Decentralized Decision Rules:** Create simple economic decision rules (e.g., "any engineer can spend $300 to save one pound of weight") to allow large teams to make aligned, system-optimal decisions without constant management oversight.

title: Evolutionary Architecture as Product @ CircleCI

From the explosive adoption of Docker to the steady rise of microservice architectures, the changing demands of software engineering teams have proven to be deeply coupled with the structure of their service–far more than they anticipated when they started the business.
